speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Silver, you're telling us a little bit about-- just a little bit about-- what it was like at Brunnlitz for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there's only one thing we got [INAUDIBLE] how you behave [INAUDIBLE] been like in a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: For myself, it was make believe more, as a concentration camp. Women and men could communicate together. We couldn't go out, eat together. I haven't got my woman. I was very depressed. Edith haven't been there. She could be there. If I would have more time in this time in Plaszow, maybe I would be able to-- to organize some legally. She would be legal. She wouldn't be killed. Because if they bring us [INAUDIBLE] we go to-- she was dressed in a men.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were really worried about Edith.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was depressed the whole time because the-- the [INAUDIBLE] people for Schindler, the-- was 400. 500. It was about 1,200. About 500, 600 was in-- in [INAUDIBLE]. Was the [NON-ENGLISH] brought them together, make their own transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over there, this people, is I was very depressed, very mad at some of them. But a lot of people got to see more about Schindler, got a grandfather, like an uncle. You know, they give him a lot of money. He'd been like own family with them. And he worried, but he brought them. He took them out in-- in-- in Auschwitz what's not simple things, and he brought them to-- to-- he brought the men in Gross-Rosen. He brought the women in-- in-- in Auschwitz. He brought them to-- it's-- it's you could say some guy who was, let's say, 30, 40, or 50. 40 years was old. You couldn't survive in a regular life-- couldn't survive, impossible. You got to be young to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There I work-- we made-- I made the first-- I worked at a very complicated machine called [NON-ENGLISH]. A very complicated machine, big like the room. You took this-- they made a big grenade. In the first operation, the machine make the piece of steel [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know, it's in English--I wish I-- make the first operation. Later they went to other machines--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you worked at a machine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if you-- you-- it was danger work. It was not hard. You just had to put the piece of steel in the-- in the machine.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had to be careful not to lose your hand or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had to be able to cut off the hand. You see? The machine went back and forth, and during this time, you had to put it in a place where the two boxes goes in.

words.text: They had to be able to

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you work at this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until I-- I was free-- they brought in this--

speaker: interviewer
text: About six months? Four months?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say from January to-- to May. May, I remember, the 5th of May. Hitler was killed, let's say. We was the-- the the Germans what had been ran the camp, and they suddenly disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the food-- was the food better at Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a way, it was the same way here. You got enough money to-- to-- once in a while, he went took-- he took maybe once a week, maybe twice a week-- he-- he got trucks. This was like a-- a private [NON-ENGLISH], like a private factory. It was not like a camp like Auschwitz, like Buchenwald, like Gross-Rosen, or like Majdanek. It was like a big factory. He himself would have lived in the factory. We were in a house. It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: He brought once in a while a half a bread. I don't know, he organized. You know, half a bread was a big thing. In Plaszow-- I told you, Edith is going to tell you. She worked in the kitchen. She saved lot of-- lot of friends in Radom. She-- she-- she brought for us is-- I don't think-- I got enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did Edith-- Edith ever come to Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. What do you mean she going to come to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she ever brought there, is what I mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She went and over there she went--

speaker: interviewer
text: She'll tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: She tells you. She went to Auschwitz. From Auschwitz she went to Ravensbruck. From Ravensbruck she bring to-- she Ravensbruck she went to-- to Sweden, to-- to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: She'll tell us that. What-- do you remember the last-- the last day, the day of liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it to us, when the world was--

speaker: interviewee
text: The liberation was in-- was with Schindler. One day he came in himself, Schindler. He took all of us. It was about 1,000, 1,100 people. And he tell the [INAUDIBLE], he told us-- we knew already. I told you I got in touch with Czech people in the end. He would finish us off, the-- the lagerfuhrer, or the Czech lady was at the [NON-ENGLISH] was watching us. She come-- you didn't have to worry. The war is to end. He told us in this time that Roosevelt was dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hitler is dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw-- we saw it's the end, because in the early in the morning, no SS, no-- no Ukraine. It's the only one thing. He was trying to save his neck himself, and we tried to save him. Is what we done. The-- the-- the-- Brunnlitz was occupied by the Russians. Really, the Russians, later the-- the Americans came-- is what the Russians would kill them for a reason. It's two gutsy boys took him. They brought him-- they brought him to the fringe zone. The Germans were still there. It was a day, two days, before the war. Nobody liked to-- to go back to the Germans when they were already free-- you find that they liked [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do towards the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do nothing. I do-- do nothing. We just waited a few days, and lot of people were-- Brunnlitz was a nice town with Czech people, and they made themselves rest or something. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: You did? You rested?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I haven't rest one minute. I just organized myself and took a rucksack with-- with-- with things that Schindler gave us. He give us. He took a few trucks. Maybe some of them are going to tell you. I don't like to say-- a Nazi's a Nazi. He did a lot of good things. He done a lot of good things. He went to Brunnlitz. Brunnlitz was a town with a factory. They made materials. And he loaded up a lot of materials, and he brought this, and he called [NON-ENGLISH]. And he say, I have enough materials for everybody, for 2,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got some material.

speaker: interviewee
text: He gave everybody got a part of the material. No materials at home. That meant a lot during the war. I brought the materials. I got tried to have money to-- to pay back [INAUDIBLE] to pay back the people, but they keep Charles. They keep the boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: You left there, and where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I walked home, from Czech to-- to-- to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it take few days. And during this time, you saw--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your health then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was-- I was-- I was younger. I was, let's say, 30 years, 31 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were strong.

speaker: interviewee
text: Strong. I was very-- I was, like I told you, the athletic type my whole life. That's the reason I survived surgery too. I was very strong, very.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had these materials. You had things with you in the rucksack--

speaker: interviewee
text: I carried the load and I walked home.

speaker: interviewer
text: You walked to Radom. And what did you find when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got there, on the-- on the-- in the station where I came back, I found that a guy from Radom came before, and he came day, looking for somebody coming. He told me my-- my brother was alive, and he had a restaurant in Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. You ask how I feel. I feel-- like I felt like-- there's no sense to say like a million dollars-- you feel like a new-- and I came home to my brother. He had already an apartment. And later it was hard to get back the-- Edith's going to tell how we got back the-- the boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Son, it took me-- took me few-- a couple of months to get him. They wouldn't give back because he was legally adopted.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you found him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I found him. They wouldn't let--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw him?

speaker: interviewee
text: After a while we have to go to court.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you saw him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw him.

speaker: interviewee
text: During this time I glimpsed him. They wouldn't let me get in there, and they run away every time I came. I showed up. I have to, and this time was very not a secure time. You've got to go with a gun. Without a gun, you've been nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a gun?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, you've got to have a gun. You know, to Poland-- ever heard the [NON-ENGLISH]? They killed the Jews. We want-- we want [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what this means? We want Poland without Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how did you support yourself during these months? How did you manage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's a good question. Dear. You have to go-- hours you have to take.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, just tell me a little summary.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you we moved in ghetto to smaller ghetto in my real place. You-- you-- you-- you know what is going on. You know you're not going to be alive. You know--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I mean now. You're back in Radom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me-- let me-- let me tell you. It's what I done. You got lot of merchandise. You gave it to Poles. You didn't know. If you came back, maybe you were like a lot the people give away to-- to-- to Poles gold, silver, and money. Nobody give back. They killed the person. They came back, they asked what they give [INAUDIBLE]. The Poles killed them, because they don't want give back. And I got-- I wouldn't go back to Poland. I was afraid. I got a-- a lady with the name [NON-ENGLISH]. She was a wonderful girl. She gave me back a part of some of the goods.

speaker: interviewer
text: I got a-- I remember their names, because they were good people-- [INAUDIBLE]. They gave back a part of-- we exchanged. What did I do? You asked a question but it's impossible. So you collected your things.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took this stuff. It went on the market, and brought us to money. And you got already the [INAUDIBLE]. You-- you-- you doing something different. I found the money, the Deutsche Reichsmark was no good in Poland. It was good when Poland was with the Germans. A part of Poland-- they made it to the Deutsche side.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got busy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got marks. Came the idea if I got marks here, Deutsche Reichsmarks, but it's not worth a penny. I accumulated-- I went to [NON-ENGLISH], and I-- why do you need that? They gave me very little. I brought this-- this money to-- to-- to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you traveled around.

speaker: interviewee
text: I traveled to Germany, and in Germany, with this Reichsmark was you could buy the whole world. Dollars, gold, silver-- [INAUDIBLE] you mind it has-- you have to work. Like there's a saying in Yiddish. You have to sleep fast, because we need the pillow. You have to do it fast. I needed money to-- to-- to pay for the boy. I need-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] We went to court, and the court give him a-- whatever you called it, I know-- to pay so-and-so much. This way I--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got back in business. It was maybe black market, whatever, buying and selling.

speaker: interviewee
text: We thought that the borders was open. You couldn't go. I went to France. I went to-- to the Germany. I went-- later we went legally to Germany, legally, because we would like to go to America, is we have to go to-- to Germany. I wouldn't stay right in Poland. You could do nothing in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Tell us in-- in just two sentences. When did Edith-- what-- how-- when did-- you find out about Edith, very quickly, and then when-- how did you get your boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. For Edith I found it as soon I saw my-- my brother. And when we was in-- together in concentration camp, is she always tried take a look if we could have the boy, because some-- some mothers and parents brought kids. Each area got different laws. They brought them. Take a look at what you done. The whole she was nagging me about why I-- I didn't try to keep the boy. So I say we should be so alive like the boy. You saw the life. It's what was happening was the orphan kids had been legally in camps. They were all murdered, and they took them away when they took the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Try to answer just quickly, though. When did you find out Edith was alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is we were in Plaszow, in-- in-- in Krakow. Is we talk-- we used to talk. We used to meet lots  of time.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I'm talking about--

speaker: interviewee
text: I've got to give you a story, I know. It's we talk if-- if-- if-- with God's will we be alive. Remember one thing-- let's meet by [INAUDIBLE]. Let's meet-- what a gentle woman-- what the keep-- we didn't know, understand she keep my brother. She keep my brother's daughter. It's let's meet over there. It's what she done. She finded us, and she got freed. She wired a-- to-- to [INAUDIBLE]. She finded us to the Red Cross, to the Red Cross that we were alive. And she wired, I'm alive. I'm in Sweden. I'm in Kristianstad. And later on we corresponded together.

speaker: interviewer
text: That must have been some-- some day for you, when you found this out. And now, in another sentence, how did you get Charles?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a big story. Let her tell, she knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, but you say it in one sentence. You-- you've got the money because you worked so hard. Then did you go through a legal process to get Charles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Legal.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got a-- [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: He's getting too emotional.

speaker: interviewee
text: You-- no, I got [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Anyway, you went through a legal process, and you did get Charles. Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we got Charles. I paid so much money. I accumulated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was Charles?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Three years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost three-- two and a half years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go? Now you're back with family.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland I organized-- it was a lot of struggle. You organized a home, where to sleep, where to live, where to do-- what is going to happen next? We knew we were not going to stay in Poland for  a million-- for millions. I could--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you have to stay in Poland, the three of you together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Together? I'd say from May to December.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went-- then we go in over there. We went to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany we went from '45 to '49, and we went to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you come in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- [LAUGHS] we came to Baltimore, Maryland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Baltimore, Maryland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I looked for a job for $0.50 an hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then how long before you moved to someplace else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Someplace else? I learned profession. It's a lot there. I had been in a lot of different businesses. I'd been in market business, food market. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Food market. Then what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had 10-- 10-- few markets. And later on, I learned the construction business. I came to the West Coast and still only one thing. I know I wouldn't do anything else.  I want construction.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came to the West Coast, and you built buildings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Buildings, homes, moved homes. I did everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year did you have another child?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Florence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Florence. And now you have Florence and Charles, and it's many years later. Did Florence and Charles have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Florence got married, yes. She have two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name-- One is [? Eric ?] and one is [? Sean. ?] They both graduated from college and doing today similar work like this. He does signs, do very wonderful signs.

speaker: interviewer
text: He does TV work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not TV. He works for a big company. I-- I don't know. When you talk with him, he's going to tell you. And Sean-- goes to college, second-year college.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you have two grandchildren from your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is [? Sean ?] and [? Eric. ?] And they have three children. [? John ?] goes to college. The second is got last year of college. And the third one is last year of high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Henry, you've lived for 50 years past the terrible times, and you've evidently lived a good life in many ways. What were the lessons? What-- what is carried along from the bad times? What has stayed with you from the bad times, and how-- how has it affected the good times?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only one, I think, to educate the people they shouldn't forget what happened-- shouldn't happen again. [SOBBING] Couldn't happen again. Even this golden America. Couldn't happen again. This picture presents my grandfather. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] His name [NON-ENGLISH], born in Poland [NON-ENGLISH]. Died short time before the war start. He died with a natural death.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year do you think this artwork was made?

speaker: interviewee
text: This artwork was made--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it 1920?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this-- no, this was a little picture. This was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, the little picture. When was it made?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was made 1920. This a reproduction. This a painting. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture present my mother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was born in 1920, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: The picture was taken in 1920. And what was her name, Henry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Nachuma Zilberszlak. Her maiden name was Nachuma [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this original taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture been taken in Radom, Poland. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture present myself. I was 18 year old. I was visiting my brother in the army, in the Polish army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give his name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Bernard Silver.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry Silver. Before, it was still Zilberszlak. We came to the United States, we changed the name to Silver.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what place was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- the picture was taken in Minsk Mazowiecki, in a military camp, a Polish military camp in-- in Minsk Mazowiecki, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what date do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: This must be-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was, let's see, 18 years old. This must be-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I can't concentrate. This is myself, Henry Silver, before Zilberszlak. The picture was taken in [INAUDIBLE], Munich. Munich was right after the war, the date--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the picture been taken 1947. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: My son Henrich Silver, born in-- born in Poland. What town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: '45, yeah. Munich, 1946. This Is my family, Edith, Charles, and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Henry Silver.

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry Silver. That's right. Edith Silver, 1949 in [INAUDIBLE]. Edith Silver and Florence Silver. She was only half a year old or something. This is my son. He's-- at the moment, he is Dr. Charles Silver, his wife [? Kathi, ?] his son [? Sasha, ?] and his daughter [? Elizabeth. ?] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it. [INAUDIBLE] Silver, Dallas, Texas, 1994. My grandson [? Eric Freeman, ?] born 1990-- The picture was taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in 1994 in Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Los Angeles. This picture been taken in Beth Shalom synagogue, with my second child of my daughter Florence, and my wife Edith, and me, Henry Silver.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's the boy's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The boy's name is [? Sean Freeman. ?] This picture been taken 1989. I'd like to introduce my two grandsons, my daughter, my wife Edith Silver, and myself. [? SEAN FREEMAN: ?] Hi, my name is a Sean Freeman. I'm 19 years old. I'm currently attending UC Santa Cruz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi, and my name is Florence Silver. I'm a realtor, and I'm not going to tell my age. [LAUGHS] [? ERIC FREEMAN: ?] My name is Eric Freeman.   I'm 22. I work in the sound department for Hyperion Animation.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Edith Silver, and that's my priceless possession, my husband, and my loving family, [? Sean, ?] [? Eric, ?] and my daughter. They're all the prime of my life. My life would be empty without them.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is very special for us to be here together. There-- there are a lot of us missing here, outside of our immediate family, my brother who-- and his family, who live in Dallas, Texas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you give their names, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Charles Silver, [? Kathi ?] Silver, [? Ariel ?] Silver, [? Sasha ?] Silver, and [? Elizabeth ?] Silver. They're really a big part that is missing, and-- and unfortunately, they can't be here. But I'm glad we have this opportunity. It's something that I've tried to do, really, in a more-- in tape form, and it just, you know, something that was put off-- the time or whatever-- it never came about. But I'm very excited about this opportunity. The Holocaust and-- and what my parents have been through has always been a part of our lives, my brother and myself. It's something that, you know, was in our hearts, even though, you know, maybe, you know, a story-- stories were told here and there, and all our families lived through this. And I really didn't know things other than I was different somehow, you know, different from other kids who I grew up with, with-- that weren't greeners-- people new to the country, people who had come here and immigrated after the war. But things never really hit me till I-- I, you know, became much older and really studied history.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fellows, what did this mean to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? SEAN FREEMAN: ?] Basically, I was just overwhelmed with respect. You know, just recently I gained a new-found respect for my culture, and I enrolled last quarter in a comprehensive Holocaust course. And I learned a lot more than I previously knew. And I went back to them, and I learned, you know, even more information. And I wrote papers, and just, I mean, the information-- there's so much to know about the Holocaust. And you know, [INAUDIBLE]. [? ERIC FREEMAN: ?] I just think it's really special having grandparents who-- who've been around and be able to survive something like this. And I tell the story to people. I tell people at work that I had to leave work early to get here today to be here to be part of this. And they're just amazed. I try and tell them the story, and you know, try and summarize and make it a little brief, but they're just amazed that-- [? SEAN FREEMAN: ?] There's no way you can summarize this. It is a part of us. It's something that we don't escape, that we don't want to escape, and we're proud to be here. It's a statement that my parents survived.
